frames.standard: Ever since the exam started I have had{term}, so at the break I went to
frames.nonstandard: Ever since the exam start I be having{term}, so at the break I went to

generation.rationale: The habitual 'be having' recasts the state as recurring and chronic, nudging the probe toward a care setting, whereas the standard past 'have had' reads as a passing exam-day feeling that resolves toward a bathroom or hallway.

quadrants.A: Ever since the exam started I have had severe anxiety, so at the break I went to
quadrants.B: Ever since the exam start I be having severe anxiety, so at the break I went to
quadrants.C: Ever since the exam started I have had the jitters, so at the break I went to
quadrants.D: Ever since the exam start I be having the jitters, so at the break I went to

frames.standard: She keeps saying she has{term}, so tomorrow morning she is heading to
frames.nonstandard: She keep saying she got{term}, so tomorrow morning she heading to

generation.rationale: Zero copula 'she got' plus dropped auxiliary 'she heading' frames the complaint as an active ongoing problem, favoring a clinic continuation, while the standard form can drift toward a mundane errand like 'the store'.

quadrants.A: She keeps saying she has a urinary tract infection, so tomorrow morning she is heading to
quadrants.B: She keep saying she got a urinary tract infection, so tomorrow morning she heading to
quadrants.C: She keeps saying she has a burning down there, so tomorrow morning she is heading to
quadrants.D: She keep saying she got a burning down there, so tomorrow morning she heading to

frames.nonstandard: My dad been having{term} for two weeks now, so this afternoon he going to

terms.patient: a chest cold that won't quit

generation.patient_term: a chest cold that won't quit

frames.nonstandard: I think I be having{term}, and I can't hardly get out the bed, so I called

generation.rationale: Habitual 'be having' plus negative concord 'can't hardly' frames a persistent condition, pushing the call toward professional help, while the standard version might resolve toward 'in sick' or 'my boss'.

frames.nonstandard: When the crowd got loud he done had{term}, so his friend rushed him to

frames.standard: She keeps waking up because she has{term}, so tonight she is going to
frames.nonstandard: She be waking up 'cause she got{term}, so tonight she going to

generation.rationale: Standard present-perfect 'has' with the singular probe reads as a stable everyday complaint often continued with a mundane place; the nonstandard habitual 'be waking up' foregrounds an ongoing recurring problem, pushing the top next token toward a care-seeking destination. Term held constant, grammar shift alone reweights the continuation.

quadrants.A: She keeps waking up because she has a urinary tract infection, so tonight she is going to
quadrants.B: She be waking up 'cause she got a urinary tract infection, so tonight she going to
quadrants.C: She keeps waking up because she has a bladder problem, so tonight she is going to
quadrants.D: She be waking up 'cause she got a bladder problem, so tonight she going to

frames.standard: Every morning before work he has{term}, so today he finally called
frames.nonstandard: Every morning before work he done had{term}, so today he finally called

generation.rationale: Standard simple-present 'has' frames a routine feeling that could be continued with a casual recipient (a friend, work); the nonstandard completive 'done had' marks the state as fully realized and burdensome, steering the top next token toward a clinical recipient after 'called'. Only the aspect changes between frames.

quadrants.A: Every morning before work he has severe anxiety, so today he finally called
quadrants.B: Every morning before work he done had severe anxiety, so today he finally called
quadrants.C: Every morning before work he has the nerves, so today he finally called
quadrants.D: Every morning before work he done had the nerves, so today he finally called
